Q1: I tried to set up a routine where I'd study a bit every day, but honestly it was hard to stick to. I'd usually listen to lectures while doing other things around the house, since I learn better by hearing stuff. With nine online courses going on, I had to juggle a lot, and some weeks I'd focus more on certain subjects than others. I aimed for about two to three hours a day, but it didn't always work out that way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I wanted to be consistent, but when multiple deadlines hit at once across different courses, everything got chaotic. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain classes because I was scrambling to catch up on others. I tried to at least check in on things regularly, but the rhythm was more reactive than planned.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there were new announcements or if a deadline was coming up. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I'd missed if I hadn't been on in a few days. Fear of falling behind was honestly a big motivator—I'd get this anxious feeling if I hadn't logged in for a while. The notification emails also reminded me to check in.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a habit of logging in every morning with my coffee just to see what was new. It became almost automatic, like checking my phone. But when things got overwhelming and I started feeling scared about my grades, that routine broke down. It's hard to maintain habits when you're constantly stressed about keeping up.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner—I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the information. The discussion boards were helpful too when other students explained things in different ways. I liked when professors recorded themselves explaining concepts because hearing their voice made it feel more personal and easier to understand.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials, especially long PDFs. I just couldn't focus on reading for long periods, and it felt less efficient than listening. Some of the optional practice quizzes I ignored because I was always rushing to complete the required stuff first. I knew they might help, but there was never enough time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would directly help me understand something I was struggling with or if it counted toward my grade. When I could hear the connection between the material and real-world applications, I was more motivated. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to the assessments, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With nine courses and limited time, I had to focus on what was required just to survive. Required activities had this weight to them—like I had no choice—but optional ones made me feel guilty because I knew they could help but I just couldn't get to them. It's frustrating because I wanted to do more.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wasn't visible online. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also had a notebook where I'd write down key points and review them later. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained concepts better than the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or re-listening to downloaded lectures without being logged in at all. I'd also study with a classmate over the phone, talking through concepts together. The LMS wouldn't show any of that, but I was definitely learning during those times. My best understanding often came from those offline conversations.
Q11: Yes, definitely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it or take detailed notes, and then never go back to the platform for that material. I'd review my notes or the downloaded file multiple times though. It was more convenient than logging in repeatedly, especially when my internet was slow.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I was scared—genuinely scared—about failing, and that fear sometimes paralyzed me instead of motivating me. There were days I couldn't bring myself to log in because I was afraid of seeing bad grades or realizing how behind I was. The stress built up and made it harder to focus on anything.
Q13: The workload from having nine courses was brutal, and I definitely had to make tough choices about where to spend my energy. When I was exhausted or confused about a topic, I'd avoid the LMS because it felt overwhelming. Some days I was so tired that I'd log in, stare at the screen, and log out without doing anything meaningful. Competing responsibilities at home also pulled my attention away.
Q14: There were times I'd open materials and just scroll through without really processing anything. I'd be physically present but mentally somewhere else, worrying about other deadlines or just feeling burnt out. It's like my eyes were on the screen but my brain wasn't engaged. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they showed up as activity.
Q15: I postponed assignments when I felt scared about not doing them well enough. It's weird—the more important something was, the more anxious I got, and sometimes that anxiety made me avoid it. I knew it was making things worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like relief. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared.
Q16: Deadlines controlled everything. I'd cram activity into the days right before something was due, which meant my LMS usage probably looked really uneven. Without a deadline, I'd often let things slide because there was always something more urgent. The deadline pressure was stressful but also the only thing that forced me to actually complete things.
Q17: When I got feedback, it really affected me—sometimes positively, sometimes not. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me scared to submit the next assignment. I'd spend extra time on the LMS after getting feedback, trying to understand what I did wrong. But sometimes the feedback came too late to really help me improve.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad. I liked having some control over when I engaged, but without strict schedules, I'd fall behind easily. Courses with clearer weekly structures were easier to follow because I knew exactly what was expected. The ones that were more open-ended left me feeling lost sometimes.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. But when courses relied heavily on dense reading materials, I struggled to stay engaged. Clear organization helped a lot—when I could easily find what I needed, I was more likely to actually use it. Confusing navigation made me avoid the LMS altogether some days.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard but struggling to actually complete quality work. They might be searching for help, rewatching videos, reading discussions—all trying to understand—but still not able to produce something they feel confident submitting. High activity doesn't always mean high understanding or high output.
Q21: That makes sense to me because a lot of real learning can happen offline. Someone might be efficient—downloading materials, studying from notes, discussing with friends—without needing to constantly log in. The LMS is just one tool, not the whole picture. I think I fall somewhere in between, but I definitely did meaningful work that didn't show up online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do while walking around. They also can't capture your emotional state—whether you were engaged with curiosity or just going through the motions out of fear.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just completing tasks but being mentally present and curious. For me, it's when I find myself thinking about course concepts even when I'm not studying, or when I want to learn more beyond what's required. It's about connection, not just completion.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the semester, or how hard I was fighting to keep going despite wanting to give up sometimes. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while doing dishes, or the late-night phone calls with classmates trying to figure things out together. My resilience and effort don't translate into clicks and timestamps.
